1:
On Revelation 3:1 — “Unto the angel of the church in Sardis write… I know thy works, that thou hast a name that thou livest, and art dead” — the witnesses mark the letter to a church alive in name only.

The Latin fathers read it of careless Christians. Victorinus: this class sets forth men “Christians only in name.” Bede: he reproves the bishop as not diligent in correcting the bad. The grammatical-historical Robertson: Sardis, old capital of Lydia, home of Croesus. Wesley: “a name that thou livest — a fair reputation, a goodly outside… but the Spirit sees the inward death.”

The verse opens the letter to Sardis. It had a reputation for life but was spiritually dead. The outward name masked an inward death.

So the witnesses see the risen Christ address Sardis as a church whose reputation outran its reality. The witnesses note the self-designation: he who “hath the seven Spirits of God, and the seven stars” — the Lord who holds the fulness of the Spirit (whence all spiritual life proceeds) and the messengers of the churches; the very titles rebuke a church that lacked the Spirit’s life. They mark the searching verdict: “thou hast a name that thou livest, and art dead” — Sardis had a fair reputation, a goodly outward show of religion, passing among men as a living church; but in Christ’s eyes, who sees the heart, it was spiritually dead. They observe the peculiar peril this names: not open heresy or persecution, but a deadness hidden under a respectable name; as Victorinus reads it, these are “Christians only in name.” They note Bede’s point that the bishop is reproved for not diligently correcting the bad. They observe Wesley’s contrast of the fair outside and the inward death the Spirit sees. They note that this is the most solemn of warnings: a church may keep its forms, its name, its standing, and yet be dead within. The verse thus opens the letter to Sardis with its piercing diagnosis: a name to live, but death in truth — the deadness for which the next verses prescribe watchfulness.
2:
On Revelation 3:2 — “Be watchful, and strengthen the things which remain, that are ready to die: for I have not found thy works perfect before God” — the witnesses mark the call to wakefulness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the urgency. Robertson: “be thou watchful” (ginou gregoron), keep on becoming wakeful. Barnes: be attentive, in contrast to the drowsy condition. Ellicott: “become of wakeful habit.” Wesley: the things which remain — knowledge, good desires, convictions — ready to die wherever pride or sloth revives.

The verse prescribes the remedy. Wake up and strengthen what remains and is dying. A dying church must rouse itself before all is lost.

So the witnesses see Christ summon the dying church to wakefulness. The witnesses note the first command: “be watchful” — or, become wakeful; not a single rousing but a settled habit of vigilance, in sharp contrast to the drowsy, sleepy condition into which Sardis had fallen. They mark the second: “strengthen the things which remain, that are ready to die” — there was still some life left, some flickering remnant of true piety — knowledge of the truth, good desires, tender convictions — but it was at the point of death; this dying remnant must be braced and confirmed before it expires utterly. They observe the searching reason: “for I have not found thy works perfect before God” — their works, though they had a name, were not complete or full before God; outwardly busy perhaps, but inwardly hollow, falling short of the sincerity God requires. They note Wesley’s point that wherever pride, indolence, or levity revives, all the fruits of the Spirit are ready to die. They observe the mercy in the command: the church is not yet wholly dead; there is something to strengthen, and time to do it. They note that the call is to immediate, vigilant action. The verse thus prescribes the cure for spiritual deadness: wake up and strengthen the dying remnant, for the works are not yet perfect before God — a rousing the next verse grounds in remembrance and repentance.

So the witnesses see Christ summon the dying church to wakefulness. The first command, “be watchful” — or, become wakeful — is no single rousing but a settled habit of vigilance, against the drowsy condition into which Sardis had fallen. The second, “strengthen the things which remain, that are ready to die,” addresses the flickering remnant of true piety — knowledge, good desires, tender convictions — at the point of death, to be braced before it expires. The searching reason: “I have not found thy works perfect before God” — not complete or full, outwardly busy but inwardly hollow. The mercy in the command is real: the church is not yet wholly dead; there is something to strengthen, and time to do it.
3:
On Revelation 3:3 — “Remember therefore how thou hast received and heard, and hold fast, and repent. If therefore thou shalt not watch, I will come on thee as a thief” — the witnesses mark the call to remember, hold fast, and repent.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the chain of commands. Robertson: “remember” (mnemoneue, keep in mind); “thou hast received” (perfect, a permanent deposit); “come as a thief” (Matthew 24:43). Adam Clarke: recall with what joy and zeal ye first heard the Gospel. Wesley: hold fast the grace received, and repent according to the word.

The verse calls Sardis back. Remember what was received, hold it fast, repent; else Christ comes as a thief. Forgetfulness invites sudden judgment.

So the witnesses see Christ press the dying church with a chain of urgent commands and a warning. The witnesses note the first: “remember therefore how thou hast received and heard” — let Sardis call to mind the truth it once received as a permanent deposit, and the joy, zeal, and earnestness with which it first heard and embraced the gospel; remembrance of better days is the spur to recovery. They mark the second and third: “and hold fast, and repent” — hold firmly whatever grace and good still remain, and turn back from the deadness into which it had drifted; memory must issue in repentance. They observe the solemn warning attached: “if therefore thou shalt not watch, I will come on thee as a thief” — should the church refuse to rouse itself, Christ will come upon it suddenly, unexpectedly, and in judgment, as a thief breaks in by night; “and thou shalt not know what hour I will come upon thee.” They note the figure’s force: the careless, sleeping church will be overtaken before it is aware. They observe that the warning of a thief-like coming is reserved precisely for the unwatchful; vigilance is the safeguard against surprise. They note that the whole appeal turns on rousing the conscience by memory and the threat of sudden visitation. The verse thus drives the call home: remember, hold fast, and repent, lest Christ come as a thief on the unwatchful church — a darkness against which the next verse sets the few who kept their garments clean.
4:
On Revelation 3:4 — “Thou hast a few names even in Sardis which have not defiled their garments; and they shall walk with me in white: for they are worthy” — the witnesses mark the faithful few.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the remnant. Robertson: “a few names” (oliga onomata), persons; “did not defile” (ouk emolynan) their garments. Ellicott: the loving eyes of the faithful witness would not ignore the good; a few had not succumbed. Wesley: though few, they had not separated; it was no virtue of the angel that they were unspotted.

The verse names the faithful remnant. A few in Sardis kept their garments undefiled and shall walk with Christ in white. Even a dead church holds some who are clean.

So the witnesses see Christ, amid the rebuke, single out the faithful few. The witnesses note the gracious “but” (in the best texts): even in so dead a church, “thou hast a few names” — a few persons — “which have not defiled their garments”; the loving eye of the faithful witness, as Ellicott observes, will not overlook the good, however small. They mark the figure: “garments” undefiled signify a character and conduct unstained by the prevailing sin and deadness; these few had kept themselves pure amid general corruption. They observe the promise: “they shall walk with me in white” — white being the color of purity, victory, and festal joy; they shall enjoy the intimate fellowship of Christ, clothed in the white robes of the redeemed. They note the ground stated: “for they are worthy” — not by their own merit absolutely, but counted worthy through grace, fit companions for the holy Christ; their faithfulness is owned. They observe Wesley’s sober note that it was no credit to the slothful angel that these few remained unspotted; their purity was their own grace, his negligence his own fault. They note the comfort: God always preserves a remnant. The verse thus honors the faithful few: the undefiled in Sardis shall walk with Christ in white, for they are worthy — a promise the next verse extends to all who overcome.
5:
On Revelation 3:5 — “He that overcometh, the same shall be clothed in white raiment; and I will not blot out his name out of the book of life, but I will confess his name before my Father, and before his angels” — the witnesses mark the threefold promise to the overcomer.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the rewards. Robertson: “shall be arrayed” (peribaleitai) in white. Adam Clarke: an allusion to registering names in a church book. Ellicott: the promise to all who conquer, not who never fell. Wesley: white the colour of victory; not blotted out, but living for ever; confessed as one of his faithful.

The verse promises the overcomer three things. White raiment, an unblotted name in the book of life, and confession before the Father. Victory is crowned with purity, security, and acknowledgment.

So the witnesses see Christ hold out to the overcomer a threefold reward. The witnesses note that the promise of walking in white, made to the faithful few, is now extended to every conqueror: “he that overcometh… shall be clothed in white raiment” — not only those who never fell, but all who battle and prevail shall wear the white robe of purity, victory, and joy. They mark the second promise: “I will not blot out his name out of the book of life” — the figure drawn from the civic and church registers of names; the overcomer’s name shall stand permanently enrolled among the living, never erased; in contrast to the dead church with its empty name, his name is secure in the register of life. They observe the third: “I will confess his name before my Father, and before his angels” — Christ will own and acknowledge the faithful conqueror in the great assembly of heaven, before the Father and the angels, as his own — the very thing he promised in the Gospels to those who confess him. They note that these three — the white robe, the unblotted name, the public confession — answer the conditions of Sardis: against deadness, life; against an empty name, an enrolled one; against shame, Christ’s own confession. They observe the assurance and dignity of the reward. The verse thus crowns the letter with promise: the overcomer is robed in white, kept in the book of life, and confessed by Christ before the Father — the hearing of which, the next verse bids the Spirit’s ear receive.
6:
On Revelation 3:6 — “He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — the witnesses mark the call to hear.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the refrain. Adam Clarke: the usual caution to attend carefully to the things spoken, in which every reader is concerned. The same formula closes each of the seven letters, calling the whole church to heed what is spoken to each.

The verse closes the letter with the standing call. Let him that hath an ear hear the Spirit. The message to Sardis is for all the churches.

So the witnesses see the letter to Sardis close with the refrain common to all seven. The witnesses note the call: “he that hath an ear, let him hear” — an appeal to the spiritually attentive, those given an ear to hear, to listen carefully and lay the message to heart. They mark that what is to be heard is “what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — the words of the risen Christ are the words of the Spirit, and though addressed to one church, they are for all the churches; each is to take warning and comfort from what is said to every other. They observe Clarke’s point that this is the usual caution to attend diligently, in which every reader is more or less concerned. They note the placement: in the letters to Sardis and the first three, this call stands before the promise; in the last three, after it — a structural feature the expositors observe, dividing the seven into two groups. They observe that the recurring summons underscores the universal relevance of these messages: the deadness, the remnant, the promises of Sardis speak to every age and assembly. They note that to have an ear is itself a gift, and to use it a duty. The verse thus seals the letter with the standing call: let every one with an ear hear what the Spirit says to all the churches — before Christ turns to address Philadelphia.

So the witnesses see the letter to Sardis close with the refrain common to all seven. The call “he that hath an ear, let him hear” appeals to the spiritually attentive to listen and lay the message to heart. What is to be heard is “what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — the words of the risen Christ are the Spirit’s words, and though addressed to one church, they are for all; each takes warning and comfort from what is said to every other. Clarke notes this is the usual caution to attend diligently, in which every reader is concerned. The placement (before the promise in the first three letters, after in the last three) divides the seven; the recurring summons underscores the universal relevance of these messages.
7:
On Revelation 3:7 — “To the angel of the church in Philadelphia write; These things saith he that is holy, he that is true, he that hath the key of David… that openeth, and no man shutteth” — the witnesses mark the titles of the holy and true Lord.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the titles. Adam Clarke: holy, in whom holiness essentially dwells; true, the fountain of truth. The Puritan Henry begins the sixth letter. Wesley: “the key of David — a prince has a key to open and shut all the doors of his palace” (cf. Isaiah 22:22).

The verse opens the letter to Philadelphia. Christ is holy, true, and holds the key of David, opening and shutting absolutely. His sovereign authority frames the letter.

So the witnesses see Christ introduce himself to Philadelphia by titles of holiness, truth, and sovereign authority. The witnesses note the first two: “he that is holy, he that is true” — the Lord in whom holiness essentially dwells and from whom all holiness flows, and who is himself the fountain and embodiment of truth, who cannot lie; these are divine titles, fitting the God of the Old Testament now claimed by Christ. They mark the third, drawn from Isaiah: “he that hath the key of David” — the key being, as Wesley explains, the token of a prince’s authority to open and shut every door of his house; Christ holds the key of David, the royal, messianic authority over the household and kingdom of God. They observe the absolute power this conveys: “he that openeth, and no man shutteth; and shutteth, and no man openeth” — what Christ opens none can close, and what he closes none can open; his decisions are final and irreversible. They note the relevance to a church of “little strength”: the Lord who holds the keys can set before it doors no enemy can shut. They observe that the titles ground the promise of the open door to follow. They note that Christ’s sovereign control over access — to the kingdom, to opportunity, to heaven — is the comfort held out. The verse thus opens the letter with the majestic self-designation: the holy and true Lord who holds the key of David and opens and shuts absolutely — the authority by which, the next verse declares, he sets an open door before Philadelphia.
8:
On Revelation 3:8 — “I know thy works: behold, I have set before thee an open door, and no man can shut it: for thou hast a little strength, and hast kept my word, and hast not denied my name” — the witnesses mark the open door set before the weak but faithful church.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the gift. Robertson: “I have set” (dedoka, perfect), “a door opened” (perfect passive); cf. 1 Corinthians 16:9; Colossians 4:3. Adam Clarke: a door to proclaim my word, which none can prevent. Wesley: little outward strength, a poor despised company, yet kept my word.

The verse commends Philadelphia. Christ sets before it an open door none can shut, for though weak it kept his word. Faithfulness with little strength wins an open door.

So the witnesses see Christ commend the faithful church and grant it an open door. The witnesses note the gift: “I have set before thee an open door, and no man can shut it” — the Lord who holds the key (v. 7) has opened for Philadelphia a door that no enemy can close; the expositors take it chiefly as a door of opportunity for the gospel, a wide entrance for proclaiming and spreading Christ’s word, though some include the door of entrance into the kingdom and glory. They mark the threefold ground of this favor. First, “thou hast a little strength” — the church was small, weak, of slender resources and reputation, a poor and despised company; yet Christ does not despise its littleness. Second, “hast kept my word” — despite its weakness it held fast the truth and obeyed. Third, “hast not denied my name” — under pressure it had not renounced Christ but confessed him. They observe the encouragement: it is not great strength but faithfulness that Christ rewards; a weak church that keeps his word and confesses his name is given an open door of usefulness. They note Wesley’s point that the little strength was outward, the faithfulness inward and real. They observe that the open door is the answer of the Lord of the keys to humble fidelity. The verse thus commends Philadelphia: weak yet faithful, it receives an open door none can shut — while its enemies, the next verse promises, will be humbled before it.
9:
On Revelation 3:9 — “Behold, I will make them of the synagogue of Satan… to come and worship before thy feet, and to know that I have loved thee” — the witnesses mark the humbling of false adversaries.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the false Jews. Robertson: “I give” (dido); these are a gift from Christ; cf. Revelation 2:9. Ellicott: “I give some” — not all are promised conversion. Wesley: I who have all power will make them bow and know that thou hast a place in my love.

The verse promises vindication. The false Jews who opposed the church shall bow before it and own that Christ loves it. Adversaries shall acknowledge the faithful.

So the witnesses see Christ promise the humbling of the church’s false accusers. The witnesses note the adversaries: those “of the synagogue of Satan, which say they are Jews, and are not, but do lie” — opponents who claimed to be the true people of God but, by rejecting Christ and persecuting his church, showed themselves to belong rather to Satan; the same false “Jews” named at Smyrna. They mark the promise: Christ will “make them… to come and worship before thy feet” — he will so work that these proud opponents shall be brought low, doing homage before the once-despised church; whether by conversion (some being savingly brought in, as Ellicott’s “I give some” suggests) or by forced acknowledgment, they shall bow. They observe the crowning point: “and to know that I have loved thee” — they shall be made to recognize that this little, scorned church is the object of Christ’s special love; the tables are turned, the despised vindicated. They note Wesley’s sense that Christ, having all power, will make the adversaries comply and own where his love rests. They observe the comfort: the faithful church, weak and reproached, is assured that its enemies will one day acknowledge Christ’s love for it. They note that vindication comes from the Lord, not from the church’s own strength. The verse thus promises the reversal: the false adversaries shall bow before the faithful and own that Christ loves them — a love that, the next verse adds, will keep them in the hour of trial.
10:
On Revelation 3:10 — “Because thou hast kept the word of my patience, I also will keep thee from the hour of temptation, which shall come upon all the world, to try them that dwell upon the earth” — the witnesses mark the promise of preservation.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the play on “keep.” Robertson: “thou didst keep… I also will keep” (etheresas…tereso), corresponding tenses; “patience” (hypomones), endurance. Ellicott: “the one who keeps God’s word is kept” — the benign requital of the kingdom; kept through, not from, the trial. Wesley: kept from the hour, so as not to enter the temptation.

The verse promises protection in trial. Because the church kept Christ’s word, he will keep it in the hour of testing coming on the world. Faithfulness is met with preservation.

So the witnesses see Christ answer faithfulness with a promise of preservation. The witnesses note the ground: “because thou hast kept the word of my patience” — the church had kept Christ’s word, the gospel that calls for patient endurance, holding fast under trial; the “word of my patience” is the teaching that requires, and Christ’s own example that models, steadfast endurance. They mark the answering promise, with its pointed play on the verb “keep”: because they kept his word, “I also will keep thee” — the one who keeps God’s word is himself kept by God, what the expositors call the gracious requital of the kingdom. They observe the scope of the coming trial: “the hour of temptation, which shall come upon all the world, to try them that dwell upon the earth” — a season of severe testing, of widespread tribulation, that would prove the inhabitants of the earth. They note the debated nuance of “keep from”: whether it means kept out of the trial altogether, or (as Ellicott urges) kept safe through it, preserved in the midst of it; the faithful are guarded either way. They observe Wesley’s reading that they would not enter the temptation as the careless would. They note the comfort to a faithful church facing coming storms: the Lord who was kept will keep them. The verse thus promises preservation: because they kept the word of his patience, Christ will keep them in the hour of trial — an assurance the next verse seals with his coming and the charge to hold fast.
11:
On Revelation 3:11 — “Behold, I come quickly: hold that fast which thou hast, that no man take thy crown” — the witnesses mark the charge to hold fast the crown.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the keynote. Robertson: “I come quickly” (erchomai tachy), the keynote of the book; “hold fast” (kratei) as in Revelation 2:25. Ellicott: continue your race as those striving for a garland (1 Corinthians 9:24). Adam Clarke: let none through your fall occupy the place God designed for you.

The verse urges perseverance. Christ comes quickly; hold fast, that none seize the crown. The prize must be kept by steadfastness to the end.

So the witnesses see Christ urge the faithful church to persevere to his coming. The witnesses note the announcement: “behold, I come quickly” — the keynote of the whole book; Christ’s coming, whether in deliverance, in providential visitation, or at the last, is near and certain; the expectation of it nerves the church to endure. They mark the charge: “hold that fast which thou hast” — keep a firm grip on the faith, the grace, the good already possessed; the church that has kept his word must keep on keeping it. They observe the motive: “that no man take thy crown” — there is a crown of life and glory appointed for the faithful, and it may be lost by failure to persevere; the warning is that another might seize the prize they let slip, or that they might forfeit it. They note Ellicott’s image of the race for a garland: they are to run on as those striving for the victor’s crown, not slackening near the goal. They observe Clarke’s solemn point that none should, by falling, let another occupy the place God designed for him. They note the balance of encouragement and warning: the coming is sure and the crown is real, but it must be held fast. They observe that perseverance, not a single victory, secures the prize. The verse thus charges the church: Christ comes quickly; hold fast the crown — the reward whose glory the next verse unfolds in the overcomer made a pillar in God’s temple.
12:
On Revelation 3:12 — “Him that overcometh will I make a pillar in the temple of my God… and I will write upon him the name of my God, and the name of the city of my God… and my new name” — the witnesses mark the promise of permanence and belonging.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figures. Robertson: “a pillar” (stylon), column, with a double sense of stability and honor (Galatians 2:9; 1 Timothy 3:15). Adam Clarke: an allusion to the pillars Jachin and Boaz, stability and strength. Ellicott: an unshaken pillar, with reference to the city’s frequent earthquakes. Wesley: fixed, beautiful, immovable; he shall go out no more.

The verse promises the overcomer permanence and identity. He shall be a pillar in God’s temple, inscribed with the names of God and the city. Stability and belonging are his reward.

So the witnesses see Christ promise the overcomer a fixed and honored place and a threefold name. The witnesses note the first figure: “a pillar in the temple of my God” — the conqueror shall be like a column in God’s temple, combining (as Robertson notes) the double sense of stability and honor; some recall the temple pillars Jachin and Boaz, “he shall establish” and “in him is strength.” They mark the special force for Philadelphia, a city shaken by frequent earthquakes that toppled its buildings: the overcomer shall be an unshaken pillar, permanently secure where earthly structures fell. They observe the promise of permanence: “and he shall go out no more” — never again to leave God’s presence, fixed forever in the heavenly temple. They note the threefold inscription: “the name of my God” (marking him as God’s own possession), “the name of the city of my God, which is new Jerusalem” (marking him a citizen of the heavenly city), and Christ’s “new name” (marking him as belonging to Christ in his exalted glory). They observe that these names declare ownership, citizenship, and intimate belonging — the overcomer wholly God’s, of the city, and of Christ. They note the contrast with the weak, shaken church now: hereafter an immovable pillar, eternally secure. The verse thus crowns the letter with its great promise: the overcomer made a permanent, honored pillar, inscribed with the names of God, the city, and Christ — the reward the Spirit’s ear, the next verse bids, should receive.
13:
On Revelation 3:13 — “He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — the witnesses mark the closing call to Philadelphia.

The grammatical-historical witnesses note the refrain, here following the promise, as in the last four letters. The recurring summons calls every hearer to heed the message to Philadelphia as for all the churches.

The verse closes with the standing call. Let him that hath an ear hear the Spirit. The promise to Philadelphia is for all.

So the witnesses see the letter to Philadelphia close, like the others, with the call to hear. The witnesses note the familiar summons: “he that hath an ear, let him hear” — the appeal to every spiritually attentive soul to listen and lay to heart what has been said. They mark that the message is “what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — not to Philadelphia only, but, through it, to all; the open door, the preservation, the crown, the pillar, the names, are held out to every faithful church and believer. They observe the structural note the expositors make: in this letter, as in the last four of the seven, the call to hear stands after the promise to the overcomer, whereas in the first three it stood before — a deliberate division of the seven into two groups. They note that the placement after the promise lets the glorious reward linger as the final word in the ear. They observe that the repeated call presses the universal and abiding relevance of these messages: what the Spirit said to Philadelphia speaks to every age. They note that to have an ear and to use it is the mark of the true disciple. The verse thus seals the letter with the standing call: let every one with an ear hear what the Spirit says to all the churches — before Christ turns to the last and gravest letter, to Laodicea.

So the witnesses see the letter to Philadelphia close, like the others, with the call to hear. The familiar summons “he that hath an ear, let him hear” appeals to every attentive soul to lay to heart what was said. The message is “what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — not to Philadelphia only, but through it to all; the open door, the preservation, the crown, the pillar, the names, are held out to every faithful church. The expositors note that here, as in the last four letters, the call stands after the promise, whereas in the first three it stood before — dividing the seven into two groups, and letting the glorious reward linger as the final word in the ear.
14:
On Revelation 3:14 — “Unto the angel of the church of the Laodiceans write; These things saith the Amen, the faithful and true witness, the beginning of the creation of God” — the witnesses mark the titles of the Amen.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the names. Robertson and Adam Clarke: “the Amen,” the True One, from aman, faithful, at once interpreted “the faithful and true witness” (Revelation 1:5). On “the beginning of the creation of God”: Clarke, “the head and governor of all creatures”; Wesley, “the Author, Prince, and Ruler of the creation.” The Puritan Henry: the worst of the seven, the reverse of Philadelphia.

The verse opens the letter to Laodicea. Christ is the Amen, the faithful witness, the beginning of creation. The God of truth addresses an untrue church.

So the witnesses see Christ introduce himself to Laodicea by titles of truth and creative supremacy. The witnesses note the first: “the Amen” — from the Hebrew word for what is firm, faithful, true; Christ is the very embodiment of certainty and faithfulness, the divine “Amen” in whom all God’s promises are sure. They mark its immediate explanation: “the faithful and true witness” — the one whose testimony never fails, fittingly named to a church full of self-deceiving falsehood. They observe the third title, which the expositors are careful to interpret rightly: “the beginning of the creation of God.” They note that this does not mean Christ was the first creature made (an error the orthodox writers reject), but, as Clarke and Wesley render it, that he is the Author, Prince, Head, and Ruler of all creation, its originating source — the active beginning from whom creation springs, not its first product. They observe the fitness of these titles to Laodicea: the church that boasted falsely of its riches is addressed by the faithful and true Witness who sees the truth, and by the Lord of all creation who can supply its real need. They note Henry’s point that Laodicea is the worst of the seven, the reverse of Philadelphia, with nothing commended. The verse thus opens the gravest letter with majestic titles: the Amen, the faithful Witness, the Beginning of creation — the God of truth confronting, in the next verses, a church of lukewarm self-deceit.
15:
On Revelation 3:15 — “I know thy works, that thou art neither cold nor hot: I would thou wert cold or hot” — the witnesses mark the rebuke of indifference.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the terms. Robertson: “cold” (psychros) and “hot” (zestos, boiling); “I would thou wert” (ophelon). Barnes: cold denotes no pretence of religion; hot, fervency. Adam Clarke: neither heathen nor Christian, listless and indifferent. Wesley: cold, an utter stranger; hot, as boiling water; so ought we to be penetrated.

The verse condemns Laodicea’s indifference. It was neither cold nor hot; Christ wishes it were one or the other. Lukewarm religion is worse than open coldness.

So the witnesses see Christ expose the besetting fault of Laodicea: a tepid indifference. The witnesses note the verdict: “I know thy works, that thou art neither cold nor hot” — Laodicea was neither frigidly irreligious nor fervently devoted, but in a middle, listless state; as Clarke puts it, neither heathen nor truly Christian, careless and undecided. They mark the startling wish: “I would thou wert cold or hot” — Christ would rather they were one extreme or the other than this in-between condition. They observe the expositors’ explanation: “cold” pictures one with no pretence of religion, openly irreligious; “hot” (literally boiling) pictures one fervent and zealous; the lukewarm is neither, having the form without the fervor. They note the paradox that even coldness is preferred to lukewarmness — not that open irreligion is good, but that the self-satisfied, half-hearted professor is harder to reach and more nauseating to Christ than the honest unbeliever, who at least makes no false claim and may yet be awakened. They observe Wesley’s sense that as boiling water, so ought the believer to be penetrated and heated by the things of God. They note that lukewarmness is the peculiar danger of a comfortable, prosperous church. The verse thus names Laodicea’s sin: a lukewarm indifference Christ would sooner see as honest coldness — an offense so great that, the next verse declares, he will spue it out.

So the witnesses see Christ expose the besetting fault of Laodicea: a tepid indifference. The verdict “neither cold nor hot” finds the church neither frigidly irreligious nor fervently devoted but in a middle, listless state — neither heathen nor truly Christian (Clarke). The startling wish “I would thou wert cold or hot” prefers either extreme to the in-between. “Cold” pictures one with no pretence of religion; “hot” (boiling) one fervent and zealous; the lukewarm is neither. Even coldness is preferred — not that open irreligion is good, but the self-satisfied half-hearted professor is harder to reach and more nauseating than the honest unbeliever; lukewarmness is the peculiar danger of a comfortable, prosperous church.
16:
On Revelation 3:16 — “So then because thou art lukewarm, and neither cold nor hot, I will spue thee out of my mouth” — the witnesses mark Christ’s loathing of lukewarmness.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “lukewarm” (chliaros, tepid); “spew thee” (emesai), to vomit, reject with disgust. Barnes and Adam Clarke: the well-known effect of tepid water on the stomach, producing nausea; an image of deep disgust. Wesley: I will utterly cast thee from me, unless thou repent.

The verse states the sentence on lukewarmness. Christ will spue the tepid church out of his mouth. Half-heartedness provokes his disgust.

So the witnesses see Christ pronounce his loathing of the lukewarm church in the strongest of images. The witnesses note the ground: “because thou art lukewarm, and neither cold nor hot” — precisely the tepid, indifferent state just named is the cause of the sentence. They mark the vivid figure: “I will spue thee out of my mouth” — the expositors point to the well-known effect of lukewarm water on the stomach, which tends to nauseate and provoke vomiting; the image conveys Christ’s intense disgust and his determination to reject the half-hearted church utterly. They observe the force of so strong a word from the Lord: lukewarm religion, which men may think respectable and safe, is to Christ nauseating, fit only to be cast out. They note that this is among the most solemn warnings in the letters: not the cold sinner but the tepid professor draws this expression of revulsion. They observe Wesley’s qualification, “unless thou repent” — the sentence is not yet executed; it is a threat to rouse, and the door of repentance remains open (as the counsel of the following verses shows). They note that the warning is meant to shock the complacent into earnestness. They observe that what Christ cannot abide is the comfortable mediocrity that has the name of religion without its heat. The verse thus delivers the sentence on lukewarmness: Christ will spue the tepid church from his mouth — a loathing that exposes, in the next verse, the self-deception beneath their boast.
17:
On Revelation 3:17 — “Because thou sayest, I am rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing; and knowest not that thou art wretched, and miserable, and poor, and blind, and naked” — the witnesses mark the self-deception of the Laodiceans.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the contrast. Robertson: a wealthy city, the church “carried the pride of wealth into its spiritual life” (Swete). Ellicott: the emphatic “the wretched one.” Adam Clarke: this deceitful conviction cut the nerves of their diligence; they rested in a false hope. Wesley: rich in their own eyes, wretched and pitiable in God’s account.

The verse exposes their delusion. They boasted of riches and need; Christ pronounces them wretched, poor, blind, and naked. Spiritual poverty hid under self-satisfied wealth.

So the witnesses see Christ tear away the Laodiceans’ self-flattering delusion. The witnesses note the boast: “thou sayest, I am rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing” — the church reckoned itself spiritually wealthy, well-furnished, self-sufficient, needing nothing; the pride of their literally wealthy city had infected their religion. They mark the source of their lukewarmness here: it is self-satisfied complacency that breeds the tepid state; thinking themselves rich, they felt no need to be earnest. They observe Christ’s devastating contradiction: “and knowest not” — their delusion was total; they were blind to their real condition. They note the fivefold verdict, each word piercing the boast: “wretched” (the emphatic “the wretched one”), “miserable” (pitiable), “poor” (against their “rich”), “blind” (against their imagined insight), and “naked” (against their fancied clothing). They observe Clarke’s point that this false conviction of safety cut the nerves of their spiritual diligence; resting in a vain hope, they made no effort. They note the terrible gap between self-assessment and reality: rich in their own eyes, bankrupt in God’s. They observe that this is the peril of the prosperous, comfortable church above all. The verse thus exposes the self-deception: boasting of wealth, they were in truth wretched, poor, blind, and naked — a destitution for which the next verse offers the true riches of Christ.

So the witnesses see Christ tear away the Laodiceans’ self-flattering delusion. The boast “I am rich, and increased with goods, and have need of nothing” reckoned the church spiritually wealthy and self-sufficient; the pride of their wealthy city had infected their religion, and complacency bred the tepid state. Christ’s devastating contradiction, “and knowest not,” exposes a total delusion, blind to their condition. The fivefold verdict pierces each boast: “wretched” (emphatic), “miserable” (pitiable), “poor” (against their rich), “blind” (against their imagined insight), “naked” (against their fancied clothing). Clarke notes this false conviction of safety cut the nerves of their diligence; rich in their own eyes, they were bankrupt in God’s.
18:
On Revelation 3:18 — “I counsel thee to buy of me gold tried in the fire… white raiment… and anoint thine eyes with eyesalve, that thou mayest see” — the witnesses mark the counsel to buy the true riches.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the irony and the remedy. Robertson: “to buy” (agorasai), almost ironical in tone. Ellicott: a touch of irony — how could the poor buy? Yet recalling Isaiah’s “without money” (Isaiah 55:1). Wesley: gold — true living faith tried in affliction; white raiment — true holiness; eyesalve — spiritual illumination.

The verse offers the cure for their poverty. Christ counsels them to buy gold, white raiment, and eyesalve from him. The truly destitute may yet be enriched by him.

So the witnesses see Christ, having exposed their poverty, graciously counsel them to receive the true riches from him. The witnesses note the tone the expositors detect: a tender irony — “I counsel thee to buy” the very things their boasted wealth could not purchase; yet the irony has no sting, for, recalling Isaiah’s invitation, the buying is “without money and without price,” received freely from Christ. They mark the threefold remedy, each answering a part of their destitution. “Gold tried in the fire” — against their poverty; true, living faith, refined in the furnace of affliction, the real spiritual wealth. “White raiment, that thou mayest be clothed” — against their nakedness; the white robe of Christ’s righteousness and true holiness, that the shame of their nakedness may not appear. “Eyesalve, that thou mayest see” — against their blindness; the anointing of the Spirit that gives spiritual sight, that they may perceive their true state and the things of God. They observe that all three are to be had only “of me” — from Christ alone; the church that thought it needed nothing is shown that it needs everything, and that everything is in him. They note Wesley’s identifications: gold is faith, raiment holiness, eyesalve illumination. They observe the mercy in the counsel: even the wretched and blind are invited to be enriched, clothed, and given sight. The verse thus prescribes the cure: buy of Christ the gold, the raiment, and the eyesalve — a counsel that springs, the next verse shows, from his rebuking love.
19:
On Revelation 3:19 — “As many as I love, I rebuke and chasten: be zealous therefore, and repent” — the witnesses mark rebuke as the proof of love.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the source. Robertson: a free rendering of Proverbs 3:12 (cf. Hebrews 12:6), with phileo for love. Ellicott: “rebuke” the word of the Spirit’s convicting, “chasten” to educate by correction; the emphatic “I.” Adam Clarke: it was love that made him reprove; be in earnest. Wesley: O how much has his unwearied love to do!

The verse reveals the heart behind the rebuke. Christ rebukes and chastens those he loves; therefore be zealous and repent. Severe words spring from love.

So the witnesses see Christ disclose the love that underlies even his sharpest rebuke. The witnesses note the principle, drawn from Proverbs: “as many as I love, I rebuke and chasten” — Christ’s rebuking and correcting of his people is not the contrary of love but its very proof; he reproves and disciplines precisely those who are dear to him, as a father corrects the children he loves. They mark the emphatic “I” the expositors note: it is Christ himself who, out of love, deals so faithfully with Laodicea. They observe the two words: “rebuke” — the convicting, conscience-piercing reproof (the word used of the Spirit’s work); and “chasten” — to discipline, to educate by correction and trial. They note the tender wonder of it after such severe words: the very church Christ threatened to spue out he assures of his love; the rebuke was an act of grace toward a beloved, erring church. They observe the charge that follows: “be zealous therefore, and repent” — against their lukewarmness, let them be fervent, earnest, on fire; and turn from their sin. They note Clarke’s point that it was love that made Christ reprove, and the cure for their tepidity is zeal. They observe Wesley’s marvel at Christ’s unwearied love toward the poor Laodicean. The verse thus reveals the loving heart behind the rebuke and calls for zeal and repentance — the door to which Christ himself stands knocking in the next verse.
20:
On Revelation 3:20 — “Behold, I stand at the door, and knock: if any man hear my voice, and open the door, I will come in to him, and will sup with him, and he with me” — the witnesses mark Christ knocking at the door.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the figure. Robertson: “I stand at the door” (hesteka, perfect), true both of Christ’s advent (James 5:9) and of the individual response (Luke 12:36). Barnes: though they had erred, the way of repentance was not closed; he is still willing. Wesley: if any open willingly, I will sup with him, delighting in what I have given.

The verse pictures Christ’s patient appeal. He stands at the door and knocks, ready to enter and sup with whoever opens. Grace still seeks the lukewarm.

So the witnesses see Christ, having rebuked and counselled, stoop to the tenderest of appeals. The witnesses note the gracious posture: “behold, I stand at the door, and knock” — the Lord who might justly have cast them out instead stands outside the door of the heart, patiently knocking, seeking entrance; though they had grieved him, the way back was not shut. They mark the breadth and individuality of the call: “if any man hear my voice, and open the door” — the appeal reaches past the church as a body to each single soul; Christ’s knock is his word and providence, and the opening is the willing reception of him by faith. They observe that the door must be opened from within; Christ knocks and calls, but does not force the latch — the response of opening is required. They note the promise of intimate fellowship: “I will come in to him, and will sup with him, and he with me” — the shared meal, the supper, the figure of closest communion and friendship; Christ will enter and feast with the one who admits him, and he with Christ. They observe Wesley’s sense that Christ delights in the very graces he himself bestows. They note the wonder that this most famous invitation is addressed to the worst of the seven churches: where sin abounded, grace yet knocks. They observe that the verse is at once a warning (Christ is outside) and a gospel (he waits to come in). The verse thus pictures the patient Saviour at the door: knocking, ready to enter and sup with whoever opens — a communion that issues, the next verse promises, in sharing his throne.
21:
On Revelation 3:21 — “To him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me in my throne, even as I also overcame, and am set down with my Father in his throne” — the witnesses mark the promise of sharing Christ’s throne.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the parallel. Robertson: “to sit” (kathisai), the saints sharing Christ’s rule (Romans 8:17). Ellicott: two thrones — the saints sit with Christ in his throne, as he sits with the Father in his. Adam Clarke: the final promise is to the conqueror. Wesley: to sit with me in unspeakable happiness and glory.

The verse crowns the letters with the highest promise. The overcomer shall share Christ’s throne, as Christ shares the Father’s. Victory ends in enthronement with Christ.

So the witnesses see Christ close the seven letters with the loftiest of all the promises: a share in his throne. The witnesses note the reward: “to him that overcometh will I grant to sit with me in my throne” — the conqueror shall be exalted to share Christ’s royal rule and glory, seated with him in highest dignity; the saints reign with the King they served. They mark the pattern set in Christ’s own experience: “even as I also overcame, and am set down with my Father in his throne” — Christ himself first overcame the world, sin, and death, and as victor was enthroned with the Father; the overcomer follows the same path from conflict to coronation. They observe the two thrones the expositors distinguish: Christ shares the Father’s throne by right of his deity and finished work; the glorified saints share Christ’s throne by grace, as his joint-heirs. They note the dignity of the promise: nothing higher could be offered than to sit with Christ where he sits. They observe that this final promise, given to the worst of the churches, holds out hope to the most fallen: even Laodicea, if it overcome, may be enthroned. They note that all the promises to the seven churches climax here, in fellowship of rule with the reigning Christ. The verse thus crowns the letters: the overcomer shall sit with Christ in his throne, as Christ sits with the Father — the Spirit’s last word to the churches, the next verse bids the ear receive.
22:
On Revelation 3:22 — “He that hath an ear, let him hear what the Spirit saith unto the churches” — the witnesses mark the final call to the seven churches.

The grammatical-historical commentators note the close. Adam Clarke (citing Wesley): this call stands before the promise in the first three letters, after it in the last four, dividing the seven into two parts. The refrain seals the whole series, calling all to hear.

The verse closes the seven letters. Let him that hath an ear hear the Spirit. The messages to all seven churches are for every hearer.

So the witnesses see the seventh letter, and with it the whole series, close on the familiar refrain. The witnesses note the call: “he that hath an ear, let him hear” — the last of seven such summons, pressing on every spiritually attentive soul the duty of heeding what has been said. They mark that the message is “what the Spirit saith unto the churches” (plural) — not to Laodicea only, but, as the close of all seven, a call to every church and believer to lay the whole series to heart. They observe the structural observation the expositors make (Wesley’s, cited by Clarke): the call to hear stands before the promise in the first three letters and after it in the last four, dividing the seven into two groups of three and four — a deliberate ordering. They note that the refrain, repeated through all seven, binds the letters together and asserts their abiding relevance: the praises, warnings, and promises spoken to these ancient Asian churches are the Spirit’s living word to the whole church in every age. They observe that as the letters end, the door opens (in the next chapter) to the vision of the throne; the call to hear the Spirit gives way to the sight of heaven. They note that to have an ear and use it is the constant mark of the disciple. The verse thus seals the seven letters with the standing call: let every one with an ear hear what the Spirit says to all the churches — before John is caught up to the open door in heaven.
